---
subtitle: ""
title: "<font style='font-size:0.75em;'>🗓️ Week 01<br/> Introduction to Computational Social Science</font>"
author: Dr [Burak Sonmez](#)
institute: '[University College London](#)'
date: 06 October 2023
date-meta: 06 October 2023
date-format: "DD MMM YYYY"
toc: true
toc-depth: 1
toc-title: "What we will cover today:"
center-title-slide: false
from: markdown+emoji
format:
  revealjs: 
    fig-responsive: true
    theme: simple
    slide-number: true
    mouse-wheel: false
    preview-links: auto
    logo: /figures/icons/course_favicon.png
    css: /css/styles_slides.css
    footer: 'SOCS0100 -- Computational Tools for Reproducible Social Science'
editor: 
  markdown: 
    wrap: 72
---

# Who am I?

## Your lecturer {.smaller}

::: columns
::: {.column style="display:inline-block;width:40%;height:60%;border-radius:1em;margin:1%;padding:1.5%;background-color:#f5f5f5"}
<figure>

<img src="/figures/people/brk_pic.jpeg" alt="Photo of Burak Sonmez" class="img-responsive atto_image_button_text-bottom" role="presentation" style="object-fit: cover;width:5em;height:5em;border-radius: 50%;font-size:1em;"/>

<figcaption><strong>Dr <a href="#" target="_blank" class="external">
Burak Sonmez</a></strong> <br/>Lecturer in Quantitative Social
Science<br/>Programme Director of BSc SSDS</figcaption>

</figure>
:::

::: {.column style="width:50%;font-size:0.85em;margin-left:3%;"}
-   PhD in Sociology (University of Essex)
-   MA in Longitudinal Social Research (University of Essex)
-   BA in Economics (Bilkent University)

[Experimental Methods]{.tag
style="background-color:var(--quarto-hl-st-color)"} <br/> [Social
Norms]{.tag style="background-color:var(--quarto-hl-fu-color)"} <br/>
[Trust]{.tag style="background-color:var(--quarto-hl-dv-color)"}
:::
:::

# Intro to CSS

## Computational Social Science {.smaller}

-   It is an interdisciplinary field that advances theories of human
    behavior by applying computational techniques to large datasets from
    social media sites, the Internet, or other digitised archives such
    as administrative records.
    
-   Our purpose is to map how such computational tools are currently
    being applied in empirical settings across social sciences.

<img src="/figures/images/css.png" alt="Number of pubs" width="600" height="600" style="display: block; margin: 0 auto;">

## Social Networks {.smaller}

-   Bail et al. (2019) use Google Search data to track the diffusion of
    cultural products across global networks. Together, these studies
    show that microlevel interactions between individuals can generate
    macrolevel diffusion patterns

<img src="/figures/images/css2.png" alt="Bail et al." width="600" height="600" style="display: block; margin: 0 auto;">

## Collective Action and Opinion Change {.smaller}

- van de Rijt's et al. leverage Change.org, an online petitioning platform, to conduct field experiments in which samples of petitions are assigned different quantities of signatures as treatments. These experiments demonstrate the importance of cumulative advantage effects in collective actions (van de Rijt et al. 2016)

-  Flores (2017) uses text data from Twitter to show how anti-immigration laws hardened public opinion against immigration in Arizona

<img src="/figures/images/css5.png" alt="Rijt et al." width="600" height="450" style="display: block; margin: 0 auto;">

## Sociology of Knowledge {.smaller}

- Drawing on data from JSTOR (one of the largest digital repositories of academic journals),  King et al. (2017) show that men are more likely to cite themselves than women —a tendency that increased over the past two decades

<img src="/figures/images/css3.png" alt="King et al." width="600" height="450" style="display: block; margin: 0 auto;">

## Virtual Reality and Social Psychology {.smaller}

-  van Loon et al. (2018) paid university students to perform collaborative tasks, but randomised half of them into a treatment condition where they used virtual reality to take the perspective of the other research participants. This intervention increased prosocial behavior

-  Virtual reality holds great promise for studying how people respond to social settings within tightly controlled experiments

<img src="/figures/images/css6.png" alt="Loon et al." width="500" height="300" style="display: block; margin: 0 auto;">

## Cultural Sociology {.smaller}

-  Salganik et al. (2006) created an online “music lab” to study how peer influence shapes musical preferences of new artists. In their experiment, participants rated music from obscure bands. In the control condition, participants received no information about its popularity, whereas those in the treatment condition were shown how many times songs were downloaded. Treated respondents were more likely to listen to songs with high download rates and to rate such songs more positively

<img src="/figures/images/css4.png" alt="Salganik et al." width="600" height="450" style="display: block; margin: 0 auto;">

## Economic Sociology and Organisations {.smaller}

- Studies indicate certain network compositions benefit women differently than men. For example, women are more likely to succeed if they are part of an inner circle within a network that is predominantly female (Yang et al. 2019)

## Demography and Population Studies {.smaller}

- Lin & Lundquist (2013) find that women are more likely to respond to dating invitations from members of dominant racial and ethnic groups, regardless of social distance

- Outside of the US context, using 58,880 hetero sexual members drawn from an original sample of 876,658 hetero sexual site users, Potârcă & Mills (2015) identify very strong racial and ethnic homogamy across European countries—and particularly those with ethnically homogeneous populations

# Git and GitHub

## Why Git? Why GitHub?  {.smaller}

- Git is a version control system. Its original purpose was to help groups of developers work collaboratively on big software projects. Git manages the evolution of a set of files – called a repository – in a sane, highly structured way

<img src="/figures/images/git1.png" alt="git" width="700" height="500" style="display: block; margin: 0 auto;">
